ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a blade-specific raw beef code; A0CKL (beef meat, raw) is the closest defensible parent code.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical raw beef blade steak serving is approximately 150 g, representing one steak portion before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, chuck, blade roast, separable lean and fat, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174033); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database entry for beef blade steak, raw
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95) as provided in the input record. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef chuck blade roast, raw (FDC ID 174033) used for cross-validation of saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrient values. Values are consistent between both sources.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates
gi_source.source_match_notes: Beef blade steak, raw contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; the numeric value 0 is used here as a sentinel to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. No GI testing is performed on pure protein-fat foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (52 mg/100 g) with high potassium (319 mg/100 g) yielding a favourable Na:K ratio of approximately 1:6
hypertension.guidance: Beef blade steak, raw is suitable for hypertensive individuals in moderate portions; sodium is low at 52 mg/100 g and potassium is high at 319 mg/100 g, but cooking methods and added marinades must avoid salt to preserve this benefit, and saturated fat intake should be managed by trimming visible fat before cooking.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrates and zero sugar; high protein (21 g/100 g) supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef blade steak, raw contains no carbohydrates or sugar and therefore has no direct glycaemic impact; its high protein content (21 g/100 g) slows gastric emptying and supports postprandial blood glucose stability, making it appropriate for type 2 diabetes management when consumed in controlled portions without sugary marinades and as part of a diet that limits total red meat frequency to reduce long-term insulin resistance risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at approximately 3.8 g/100 g and cholesterol at 47 mg/100 g; red meat classification with associated cardiovascular risk at high frequency
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Beef blade steak, raw contains approximately 3.8 g saturated fat and 47 mg cholesterol per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit red meat consumption to no more than 2–3 times per week, trim visible fat before cooking, avoid frying in saturated fats, and ensure overall dietary saturated fat remains within recommended limits (less than 10% of total energy) to manage LDL-cholesterol and cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid (omega-6) content in red meat amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Beef blade steak is a red meat containing arachidonic acid that amplifies RA inflammatory pathways; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit red meat to no more than once per week, pair with anti-inflammatory foods (turmeric, ginger, leafy greens), and consider replacing with oily fish or lean poultry to obtain protein without the pro-inflammatory omega-6 load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef blade steak, raw, is an uncooked cut of beef taken from the shoulder blade (chuck) region of the animal, typically sold bone-in with lean muscle meat and both internal and external fat attached. The cut displays a deep red colour with visible marbling throughout the muscle fibres, indicating moderate intramuscular fat content. It is a versatile, economical cut used across Western cuisines for grilling, braising, slow-cooking, and pan-frying. Per 100 g raw weight, it provides 169 kcal, 21 g protein, 9.5 g total fat (approximately 3.8 g saturated), and 0 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, zero-carbohydrate food suitable for ketogenic, low-carbohydrate, and high-protein dietary patterns. Sodium is low at 52 mg/100 g and potassium is high at 319 mg/100 g, supporting a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio beneficial for blood pressure management. Iron content is 1.83 mg/100 g (haem iron, highly bioavailable), and zinc is approximately 4.5 mg/100 g, supporting immune function and wound healing. Cholesterol is 47 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is not applicable as the food contains negligible carbohydrates. Clinically, this cut is appropriate for type 2 diabetes and hypertension management in moderate portions, but should be consumed with caution by individuals with cardiovascular disease or rheumatoid arthritis due to saturated fat and arachidonic acid content respectively. Halal-certified when sourced from approved abattoirs.

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from Singapore HPB Foodo database (confidence 0.95, reviewed status) with cross-validation against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for beef chuck blade roast, raw. Both approved sources are consistent. Saturated fat, fatty acid profile, and micronutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy as HPB does not report these fields; values are consistent with the cut type and fat content.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as beef blade steak is a pure animal muscle and fat product containing no plant-derived carbohydrates; confirmed zero per Singapore HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central. Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as animal muscle tissue contains no dietary fibre. Saturated fat estimated at 3.8 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174033 (beef chuck blade roast, raw) as HPB source does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is consistent with the reported total fat of 9.5 g/100 g. Monounsaturated_fat_g (4.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.4), and trans_fat_g (0.3) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions. Vitamin C and Vitamin A set to 0.0 as beef muscle meat contains negligible amounts confirmed by USDA SR Legacy. All other micronutrient values (B vitamins, minerals) sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for beef chuck blade roast, raw, as HPB does not report these fields. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates; gi_category set to not_applicable and glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. The glycemic_index field is set to the numeric value 0 to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric, with gi_source and gi_category documenting the not-applicable rationale. Halal status applies when sourced from an approved halal-certified abattoir. Kosher status not confirmed. The occasion_tag 'breakfast' from the input cultural data is retained in meal context but is non-standard for raw steak; primary meal context is dinner/lunch.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to numeric value 0 to satisfy the requirement that glycemic_index must be numeric. gi_category set to not_applicable. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source documents the not-applicable rationale referencing University of Sydney GI Database.
